item.title: The Summer We Went Back to the Movies
item.description: For the first time since the pandemic shuttered cinemas the world over, Hollywood has got its mojo back.

item.title: Aegea Investors, Equatorial Bid in One of Brazil’s Biggest Deals This Year
item.description: Two groups submitted bids to become the strategic investor in Copasa, advancing the Minas Gerais state government’s plan to privatize one of Brazil’s largest water and sewage utilities, according to people familiar to the matter.

item.title: Oil Holds Decline as Traders See Progress Toward Iranian Deal
item.description: Oil held a drop on signs that negotiations to extend a US-Iranian ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz were making progress.

item.title: New Zealand’s Central Bank Set to Hold Rates and Stay in ‘Wait and See’ Mode
item.description: New Zealand’s central bank is expected to keep its key interest rate unchanged on Wednesday, opting to look through the inflationary impact of the Middle East energy shock in favor of supporting a fragile economic recovery.

item.title: Hungary Sees May 28 Deal With EU on Frozen Funds, PM Magyar Says
item.description: Hungarian Prime Minister Peter Magyar said he’ll sign a political accord with the head of the European Union’s executive on May 28 on the release of the bloc’s frozen funds.

item.title: Vigilant ECB Hasn’t Yet Seen Second-Round Effects, Villeroy Says
item.description: A spike in energy costs hasn’t yet turned into second-round effects in other parts of the euro-area economy, according to outgoing European Central Bank Governing Council member Francois Villeroy de Galhau.

item.title: Mexico to Host Iran Players for World Cup as War Tensions Simmer
item.description: Mexico will serve as the Iranian national team’s World Cup base after US authorities refused to let the players stay overnight on its soil amid ongoing military tensions.

item.title: Ghana to Start Buying 30% of Large Gold Mines’ Ouput From June
item.description: Ghana’s central bank said it plans to increase gold purchases from the West African nation’s large-scale producers to 30% of their output from 20%, starting June 1.

item.title: Cox Group Took 54% Bridge Loan for Deal to Buy Iberdrola Mexico
item.description: Spanish utility Cox ABG Group SA took a 54% bridge loan to help fund a $4.2 billion asset acquisition in Mexico and placed shares as collateral.

item.title: Mexico Exports Rise to Their Highest Level on Record in April
item.description: Mexico’s trade surplus widened far more than expected in April to $4.52 billion, well above the median estimate of $476.35 million in a Bloomberg survey, as exports climbed to the highest since the data began in 1980. The surge highlights how the country continues to strengthen its role as a manufacturing hub for the US economy as Washington takes a tougher stance on trade. The cumulative trade surplus for the first four months of the year reached $3.51 billion, reversing from a $314 million def

item.title: Czech Premier Urges Interest Rate Cut as Inflation Risks Grow
item.description: The Czech Republic’s prime minister urged the central bank to cut interest rates, doubling down on his push for lower borrowing costs even as the country faces growing inflation risks from energy prices.

item.title: Mozambique Holds Key Rate as Fuel Shock Clouds Inflation Outlook
item.description: Mozambique’s central bank kept its benchmark interest rate unchanged for the second time in a row as surging global energy costs push local prices higher and muddy the inflation outlook.

item.title: Top Bauxite Producer Guinea to Unveil Export Controls in June
item.description: Guinea, the world’s biggest bauxite producer, plans to announce reforms next month to control exports of the ore used in making aluminum as it bids to bolster prices.

item.title: Nigeria’s First Quarter GDP Eases on Slowdown in Oil Sector
item.description: Nigeria’s economy grew at a slower-than-expected pace in the first quarter as growth in both its oil and non-oil sectors eased.

item.title: Top Africa Bourse to Tighten Algo-Trading, Market-Access Rules
item.description: Africa’s biggest stock-exchange operator is working to tighten oversight of how brokers and trading firms access the bourse to lessen the risk of rogue algorithms and trading errors that could disrupt the market.

item.title: Petrol Tourists Flock to Poland in Search of Cheap Fill Ups
item.description: The Polish government’s program to cap fuel prices at the pump is enticing drivers from nearby countries to cross the border and fill up with the some of the cheapest petrol in the European Union.

item.title: India’s Top Officials Rally Behind Economy as Oil Crisis Bites
item.description: India’s top officials sought to allay concerns about the economy as soaring oil prices roiled financial markets and the currency and weighed on growth prospects.

item.title: Israel Cut Interest Rates as Interim US-Iran Deal Edges Closer
item.description: Israel’s central bank resumed interest rate cuts on Monday, encouraged by stabilizing inflation, a stronger shekel and signs that the US and Iran are edging closer to a deal to extend their ceasefire.

item.title: Prosus Asks EU to End Forced Sale of Delivery Hero Stake
item.description: Prosus NV has asked the European Union to drop a requirement that it sell shares in Delivery Hero SE, part of antitrust approval for its acquisition of another delivery platform, people familiar with the matter said.

item.title: Bangladesh Seeks New IMF Program as Reform Challenges Mount
item.description: Bangladesh is pushing for a fresh lending arrangement with the International Monetary Fund to replace an existing $5.5 billion bailout program that the government says was “designed for a different economic reality.”

item.title: Iran War Squeezes India’s Gas Power Supply as Demand Hits Record
item.description: India’s gas power generation has plunged to the lowest level in at least six years, with the Iran war hitting fuel shipments, straining electricity supplies at a time when a scorching summer is pushing demand to record highs.

item.title: Italian Stocks Hit First Record in 26 Years Led by Energy, Chips
item.description: Italy’s benchmark equity index rose past its all-time closing high set in 2000, with a recent rally in energy and chip stocks supercharging it to record levels.

item.title: Kenya Treasury Warns Budget Strain May Force Spending Cuts
item.description: Kenya may be forced to cut spending in its next fiscal budget as rising debt-service costs, limited tax options and tighter borrowing constraints strain the Treasury’s financing plans.

item.title: Zepto Said to Plan June Public Filing for $1 Billion India IPO
item.description: Rapid-commerce firm Zepto Ltd. is preparing to publicly file in the first half of June for an initial public offering that may raise up to $1 billion, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Pope Says AI Should Be Disarmed to Avoid Dominating Humanity
item.description: Pope Leo XIV said artificial intelligence should be “disarmed” to protect humanity from its dangers, adding his voice to a heated debate over the extent to which governments should regulate a technology that is reshaping the world.

item.title: Turkey Rate Hike Bets Rise as Political Noise Adds to Iran Woes
item.description: Investors are increasing bets that Turkey’s central bank will need to raise interest rates, as higher energy costs keep inflation elevated and a political crisis puts pressure on the lira.

item.title: Japan Won’t Add Bonds on Calendar Basis to Fund Extra Budget
item.description: Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said the government will finance its extra budget without increasing bond issuance on a calendar basis, in an attempt to ease market concerns over government finances.

item.title: Swiss Trader Had Lucrative Role Getting Iraqi Oil Through Hormuz
item.description: A little-known Swiss trading company played a key role in the transit through the Strait of Hormuz of an oil supertanker whose stop-start journey captivated the oil market earlier this month, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Ericsson Quits Suburb Once Touted as Sweden’s Silicon Valley
item.description: Ericsson AB is moving its global headquarters to central Stockholm after more than two decades in Kista, a struggling suburb in the Swedish capital once touted as Sweden’s Silicon Valley.

item.title: BlackRock’s Saigal Sees ‘Sufficient Factors’ to Justify Fed Cut
item.description: The Federal Reserve may have enough reason to justify an interest rate cut rather than a hike under new chairman Kevin Warsh, according to BlackRock Inc.
